core_identity.title: The Four Rules of Fractions (Add, Subtract, Multiply, Divide)
core_identity.author: Gerry Docherty

curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.subtopic: four operations with fractions (including mixed numbers, simplifying, reciprocals, LCD)
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Use equivalent fractions and the lowest common denominator to add/subtract, multiply and divide fractions accurately (including mixed numbers), and apply fraction rules to fair, safe real-life decisions such as rationing and recipes. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

verse_ality.driving_question: How can we use the four rules of fractions to make fair and accurate decisions in real life?

knowing_world.focus: Learn and practise how to add, subtract, multiply, and divide fractions with accuracy and confidence, including simplifying, using LCD for addition/subtraction, and using Keep–Change–Flip for division.
key_concepts: Adding fractions with a common denominator
Subtracting fractions with a common denominator
activities: Tiny worked examples: add, subtract, multiply (including cross-cancel), and divide (including converting to a mixed number).
Keyboard-friendly fraction entry practice using / when typing.

human_story.focus: Fractions show up in rationing, recipes, fair pay, and resource sharing. Comparing fractional allocations helps people argue for fairness when communities receive different shares of the same resource.
justice_prompts: If two communities receive different fractional amounts of the same resource, how can we compare and argue for fair distribution?
activities: Guest voice invitation: find a short clip or quote from a chef, nurse, or engineer who uses fractions, and explain how precision keeps people safe or ensures fairness.

expressive_self.focus: Use creative expression to make fraction rules memorable and to practise clear explanation for future learners.
activities: Sketch a ‘fraction machine’ showing what happens for +, −, ×, ÷ (labels only).
Write a 3–4 line poem/rap using “Keep–Change–Flip” and “Find the LCD” as hooks.
Record a 30–60s voice note explaining one worked example step-by-step.

designed_world.focus: Use an interactive Fraction Rules Sandbox to practise all four rules, model with fraction bars, and solve a design brief where fractional precision supports accessibility and safety.
tools: Canva embed: Fraction Rules Sandbox (interactive practice with modelling)
activities: Warm-up: enter 2/3 + 3/4 and 5/12 − 1/8; check LCD each time.
Fluency: create three × and three ÷ fraction problems; solve, simplify, and show cross-cancelling if used.
Reasoning: recipe scaling using 3 1/2 cups for 12 people; find per-person fraction and new amount for 8 people.
Ethical design prompt: design a measuring aid for someone with one hand or low vision to portion 3/4, 1/3, and 1/8 accurately (shape, grip, tactile/high-contrast markings, tolerance).
Buddy challenge: swap one × and one ÷ question; check steps and write a one-line ‘fix-it rule’ when errors are found.
design_challenges: Design a measuring aid for fractional accuracy (3/4, 1/3, 1/8) that supports one-handed use and/or low vision, explaining how markings and shape reduce error.

regulation_supports: Box breath 3–4 cycles (inhale 4, hold 4, exhale 4, hold 4).
Hand stretch: open–close fists ×8; roll shoulders ×5 each way.
Pace break: stand, look 20m away for 20s; sip water.

pathways.name: Final Task - Fraction Rules Guide
pathways.focus: Create a one-page guide demonstrating all four fraction rules with worked examples, a designed real-life context problem, and a short reflection on a reliable checking strategy.
products: Two worked examples for each rule (+, −, ×, ÷)
One real-life context problem you design and solve (multi-step, unfamiliar)
A 3–4 sentence reflection on your most reliable strategy (LCD checklist, Keep–Change–Flip, cross-cancel, etc.)
assessment_criteria: Conceptual Understanding — Shows why methods work (LCD reasoning; why cross-cancelling is valid; why reciprocals turn division into multiplication)
Fluency & Accuracy — Minimal notation errors; correct simplification; clear mixed ↔ improper conversions
Application — Includes and solves at least one unfamiliar, multi-step word problem (scaling, fair-sharing, etc.)
Independence & Reflection — States a strategy, one mistake caught, and how to avoid it next time

required: Understand numerator and denominator and represent fractions
Find equivalent fractions by scaling numerator and denominator
Know basic multiplication/division facts for simplifying
Comfort converting between mixed numbers and improper fractions
